title: Ascent, round 14: retake B or save?

source.note: MOCK DATA — hand-authored fixture in HenrikDev match shape. A real deployment builds scenarios like this from the signed-in user's own recent matches via debrief-fetch-valorant-match; numbers below are realistic but not from a real match.

presented_to_user.role: You are the in-game leader on the defending side, playing Omen.
presented_to_user.situation: Ascent, round 14 of a competitive match. Score 7–6 in your favor, first round of the second half was yours. The spike just went down on B site with 38 seconds left in the round. You lost both B defenders in the site hit. Three of you are alive: you (Omen, mid), your Jett (A site, operator), and your Killjoy (rotating from A, rifle). The attackers have four alive and post-planted positions unknown — at least two confirmed on site, one seen lurking market.
known_facts: Your economy: you have 2,900 credits, Jett has 4,200 with an operator, Killjoy has 3,700 with a Vandal. Team bank next round is healthy if you save.
Their economy: attackers just spent big — this is their gun round; if they take it they hit 8–7 with full guns and you likely face them again on a rebuy.
Spike planted default on B with 38 seconds on the round clock; defuse takes 7 seconds (3.5 to half).
You have Omen smokes (both up) and a flash; Killjoy has her ult (lockdown) available but it takes 13 seconds to detain.
Retaking B means crossing market or mid — the lurker was last seen market 8 seconds ago.
A 3v4 retake into a planted spike is statistically unfavorable; saving guarantees full loadouts next round at 7–7.
presented_to_user.question: Do you commit all three to the B retake, or call the save and concede the round? Explain your call — what are you weighing, what are you betting on, and what would make you wrong?

ground_truth.actual_decision: The team called the save. All three players held A/mid positions, denied exit frags, and conceded the round 7–7 with full loadouts and Killjoy ult still banked.
ground_truth.actual_outcome: The save paid off across the next two rounds: at 7–7 the defenders won a full-buy round on the back of the preserved operator and Killjoy ult (lockdown forced the attackers off B before contact), then won the follow-up against a broken attacker economy to go 9–7. The attackers' round-14 gun round win gave them one round, not a run. Post-match win probability models put a 3v4 retake into a default B plant with an unknown market lurker at roughly 22–28%; the save line preserved an estimated 61% win chance for round 15.
decision_rationale_factors: The retake math: 3v4 into a planted spike needs you to win multiple duels AND secure a 7-second defuse window. With a lurker cutting market, the effective entry is into crossfires; ~25% round equity against giving up guns worth ~10,900 combined credits.

Ult economics: Killjoy lockdown is worth far more on a buy round you control (forcing site control cleanly at 7–7) than burned on a low-percentage retake where its 13-second detain exceeds comfortable clock management after crossing mid.
Score state: 7–6 up means the save keeps you no worse than even with guns; losing a forced retake makes it 6–8 in effect — down a round AND down loadouts, the classic double-loss.
Information state: two attackers confirmed on site, positions of the other two unknown. Retakes are won on information advantage; here the attackers hold it.
The operator is the single most valuable item on the map for the defense on Ascent A; feeding it into a B retake crossfire converts a durable structural edge into a coin flip.
ground_truth.defensible_alternative: Committing to the retake is not indefensible IF the user builds it on the right levers: using both Omen smokes to cut the site crossfire, flashing the market lurker's angle, and accepting the fight only on a fast hit before post-plant setups settle (within ~10 seconds). A user who calls the retake but prices the ~25% equity, commits utility in the right order, and names the save as the fallback if first contact is lost is showing sound process with a higher risk appetite. They should score well on reasoning, less on calibration — the numbers here genuinely favored the save.
common_mistakes: Vibes-only aggression: 'we can win any fight' with no engagement with player count, clock, utility, or economy.
Ignoring the lurker — retake calls that path through market as if it were clear.
Treating the save as 'giving up' rather than pricing the next-round win probability it buys.
Burning Killjoy ult in the retake without noting its 13-second detain barely fits the remaining clock after the rotate.
No falsifier: never stating what information would flip the call (e.g. seeing the lurker rotate onto site makes market crossing safe).

verified_data_points.note: MOCK values, internally consistent, in the shape a HenrikDev round payload supports.
